exercises.prompt: The greenlight board wants each genre's top slate. Among movies with at least 50 ratings and a non-null genre1, rank films within their genre by avg_rating descending, breaking ties by num_ratings descending, then title ascending, then movie_id ascending. Keep each genre's top 5 and return genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings, and slate_status (CASE: 'Flagship' for the genre's number 1, 'Greenlight' for ranks 2 and 3, otherwise 'Shortlist'). Order by genre1 ascending, then the within-genre rank ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.title, m.genre1, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL), ranked AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM eligible) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings, CASE WHEN genre_rank = 1 THEN 'Flagship' WHEN genre_rank <= 3 THEN 'Greenlight' ELSE 'Shortlist' END AS slate_status FROM ranked WHERE genre_rank <= 5 ORDER BY genre1, genre_rank
hints: You can't filter on a window's output in the same SELECT, so stage it. An eligible CTE joins movies to ratings and applies the num_ratings >= 50 and non-null genre1 filters. A ranked CTE adds ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) so each film carries its within-genre position. The outer query keeps rank <= 5 and turns the rank into a slate_status with CASE.

WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.title, m.genre1, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL), ranked AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM eligible) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings, CASE WHEN genre_rank = 1 THEN 'Flagship' WHEN genre_rank <= 3 THEN 'Greenlight' ELSE 'Shortlist' END AS slate_status FROM ranked WHERE genre_rank <= 5 ORDER BY genre1, genre_rank.

```sql
WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.title, m.genre1, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL), ranked AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM eligible) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings, CASE WHEN genre_rank = 1 THEN 'Flagship' WHEN genre_rank <= 3 THEN 'Greenlight' ELSE 'Shortlist' END AS slate_status FROM ranked WHERE genre_rank <= 5 ORDER BY genre1, genre_rank
```

exercises.prompt: The studio wants to see how Action, Comedy, and Drama have trended across release decades. Consider only movies with a non-null year and at least 30 ratings whose genre1 is 'Action', 'Comedy', or 'Drama', and bucket each into its decade as year - (year % 10). For every (genre1, decade) return genre1, decade, film_count (movies in that bucket), mean_rating (average of avg_rating, rounded to 2 decimals), change_vs_prev (this decade's mean_rating minus the previous decade's mean_rating within the same genre, rounded to 2 decimals — NULL for a genre's earliest decade), and direction (CASE: 'baseline' when there is no previous decade, otherwise 'up', 'down', or 'flat' comparing this mean_rating to the previous one). Order by genre1 ascending, then decade ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH pool AS (SELECT m.genre1, m.year - (m.year % 10) AS decade, r.avg_rating FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.year IS NOT NULL AND m.genre1 IN ('Action', 'Comedy', 'Drama')), by_decade AS (SELECT genre1, decade, COUNT(*) AS film_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS mean_rating FROM pool GROUP BY genre1, decade), trend AS (SELECT genre1, decade, film_count, mean_rating, LAG(mean_rating) OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY decade) AS prev_mean FROM by_decade) SELECT genre1, decade, film_count, mean_rating, ROUND(mean_rating - prev_mean, 2) AS change_vs_prev, CASE WHEN prev_mean IS NULL THEN 'baseline' WHEN mean_rating > prev_mean THEN 'up' WHEN mean_rating < prev_mean THEN 'down' ELSE 'flat' END AS direction FROM trend ORDER BY genre1, decade
hints: Three CTEs. pool joins movies to ratings and keeps the three genres with a year and num_ratings >= 30, deriving decade as year - (year % 10). by_decade groups pool by genre1 and decade for film_count and mean_rating (ROUND the average to 2). trend adds LAG(mean_rating) OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY decade) so each row sees the prior decade's mean; the outer query subtracts for change_vs_prev and labels direction with CASE, treating a NULL previous as 'baseline'.

WITH pool AS (SELECT m.genre1, m.year - (m.year % 10) AS decade, r.avg_rating FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.year IS NOT NULL AND m.genre1 IN ('Action', 'Comedy', 'Drama')), by_decade AS (SELECT genre1, decade, COUNT(*) AS film_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS mean_rating FROM pool GROUP BY genre1, decade), trend AS (SELECT genre1, decade, film_count, mean_rating, LAG(mean_rating) OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY decade) AS prev_mean FROM by_decade) SELECT genre1, decade, film_count, mean_rating, ROUND(mean_rating - prev_mean, 2) AS change_vs_prev, CASE WHEN prev_mean IS NULL THEN 'baseline' WHEN mean_rating > prev_mean THEN 'up' WHEN mean_rating < prev_mean THEN 'down' ELSE 'flat' END AS direction FROM trend ORDER BY genre1, decade.

```sql
WITH pool AS (SELECT m.genre1, m.year - (m.year % 10) AS decade, r.avg_rating FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.year IS NOT NULL AND m.genre1 IN ('Action', 'Comedy', 'Drama')), by_decade AS (SELECT genre1, decade, COUNT(*) AS film_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS mean_rating FROM pool GROUP BY genre1, decade), trend AS (SELECT genre1, decade, film_count, mean_rating, LAG(mean_rating) OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY decade) AS prev_mean FROM by_decade) SELECT genre1, decade, film_count, mean_rating, ROUND(mean_rating - prev_mean, 2) AS change_vs_prev, CASE WHEN prev_mean IS NULL THEN 'baseline' WHEN mean_rating > prev_mean THEN 'up' WHEN mean_rating < prev_mean THEN 'down' ELSE 'flat' END AS direction FROM trend ORDER BY genre1, decade
```

exercises.prompt: Which descriptive tags mark the best-reviewed films? Join tags to movies and ratings, keeping only movies with at least 20 ratings. For each tag applied to at least 8 such movies, return tag, movie_count (how many qualifying movies carry it), mean_rating (average of those movies' avg_rating, rounded to 2 decimals), and quality_rank = RANK() OVER (ORDER BY mean_rating DESC) with tied tags sharing a rank. Order by mean_rating descending, then tag ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH tagged AS (SELECT t.tag, r.avg_rating FROM tags t JOIN movies m ON t.movie_id = m.movie_id JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 20), tag_stats AS (SELECT tag, COUNT(*) AS movie_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS mean_rating FROM tagged GROUP BY tag HAVING COUNT(*) >= 8) SELECT tag, movie_count, mean_rating, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY mean_rating DESC) AS quality_rank FROM tag_stats ORDER BY mean_rating DESC, tag
hints: The tags table holds one row per (movie, tag), so a three-table join to movies and ratings — filtered to num_ratings >= 20 — gives one row per tagged qualifying movie. A tagged CTE builds that set carrying tag and avg_rating; a tag_stats CTE groups by tag with COUNT(*) AS movie_count and ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS mean_rating, keeping only tags with HAVING COUNT(*) >= 8. The outer query ranks by mean_rating.

WITH tagged AS (SELECT t.tag, r.avg_rating FROM tags t JOIN movies m ON t.movie_id = m.movie_id JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 20), tag_stats AS (SELECT tag, COUNT(*) AS movie_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS mean_rating FROM tagged GROUP BY tag HAVING COUNT(*) >= 8) SELECT tag, movie_count, mean_rating, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY mean_rating DESC) AS quality_rank FROM tag_stats ORDER BY mean_rating DESC, tag.

```sql
WITH tagged AS (SELECT t.tag, r.avg_rating FROM tags t JOIN movies m ON t.movie_id = m.movie_id JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 20), tag_stats AS (SELECT tag, COUNT(*) AS movie_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS mean_rating FROM tagged GROUP BY tag HAVING COUNT(*) >= 8) SELECT tag, movie_count, mean_rating, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY mean_rating DESC) AS quality_rank FROM tag_stats ORDER BY mean_rating DESC, tag
```

exercises.prompt: The board wants each major genre benchmarked against the catalog as a whole. Among movies with at least 30 ratings and a non-null genre1, compute per-genre stats and keep only genres with at least 20 qualifying films. For each, return genre1, film_count, genre_mean (average avg_rating, rounded to 2 decimals), catalog_mean (the average of the kept genres' genre_mean values via AVG(...) OVER (), rounded to 2 decimals), vs_catalog (genre_mean minus that catalog average, rounded to 2 decimals), and standing (CASE: 'above' when the genre's mean is greater than or equal to the catalog average, otherwise 'below'). Order by genre_mean descending, then genre1 ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.genre1, r.avg_rating FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL), genre_stats AS (SELECT genre1, COUNT(*) AS film_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS genre_mean FROM eligible GROUP BY genre1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 20) SELECT genre1, film_count, genre_mean, ROUND(AVG(genre_mean) OVER (), 2) AS catalog_mean, ROUND(genre_mean - AVG(genre_mean) OVER (), 2) AS vs_catalog, CASE WHEN genre_mean >= AVG(genre_mean) OVER () THEN 'above' ELSE 'below' END AS standing FROM genre_stats ORDER BY genre_mean DESC, genre1
hints: Stage the per-genre figures first, then let a partitionless window supply the catalog benchmark. eligible carries genre1 and avg_rating for movies with num_ratings >= 30 and a non-null genre1. genre_stats groups it into film_count and genre_mean (ROUND the average to 2), keeping genres with HAVING COUNT(*) >= 20. In the outer query, AVG(genre_mean) OVER () — an empty OVER () spanning every kept genre — is the catalog_mean you compare each genre against.

WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.genre1, r.avg_rating FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL), genre_stats AS (SELECT genre1, COUNT(*) AS film_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS genre_mean FROM eligible GROUP BY genre1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 20) SELECT genre1, film_count, genre_mean, ROUND(AVG(genre_mean) OVER (), 2) AS catalog_mean, ROUND(genre_mean - AVG(genre_mean) OVER (), 2) AS vs_catalog, CASE WHEN genre_mean >= AVG(genre_mean) OVER () THEN 'above' ELSE 'below' END AS standing FROM genre_stats ORDER BY genre_mean DESC, genre1.

```sql
WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.genre1, r.avg_rating FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 30 AND m.genre1 IS NOT NULL), genre_stats AS (SELECT genre1, COUNT(*) AS film_count, ROUND(AVG(avg_rating), 2) AS genre_mean FROM eligible GROUP BY genre1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 20) SELECT genre1, film_count, genre_mean, ROUND(AVG(genre_mean) OVER (), 2) AS catalog_mean, ROUND(genre_mean - AVG(genre_mean) OVER (), 2) AS vs_catalog, CASE WHEN genre_mean >= AVG(genre_mean) OVER () THEN 'above' ELSE 'below' END AS standing FROM genre_stats ORDER BY genre_mean DESC, genre1
```

exercises.prompt: For the final greenlight vote, the studio blends critical rating with audience reach into one score. Among movies with at least 50 ratings whose genre1 is 'Action', 'Comedy', 'Drama', 'Crime', or 'Adventure', compute score = ROUND(avg_rating + 0.3 * LN(num_ratings), 3). Rank films within their genre by score descending, breaking ties by num_ratings descending, then title ascending, then movie_id ascending; keep each genre's top 5. Return genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings, score, and verdict (CASE: 'Greenlight' for ranks 1 and 2, 'Develop' for ranks 3 through 5, otherwise 'Hold'). Order by genre1 ascending, then the within-genre rank ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.title, m.genre1, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action', 'Comedy', 'Drama', 'Crime', 'Adventure')), scored AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, ROUND(avg_rating + 0.3 * LN(num_ratings), 3) AS score FROM eligible), ranked AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, score, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY score DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM scored) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings, score, CASE WHEN genre_rank <= 2 THEN 'Greenlight' WHEN genre_rank <= 5 THEN 'Develop' ELSE 'Hold' END AS verdict FROM ranked WHERE genre_rank <= 5 ORDER BY genre1, genre_rank
hints: Build the score in its own step so the ranking window can order by it. eligible applies the num_ratings >= 50 floor and the five-genre filter; scored adds score = ROUND(avg_rating + 0.3 * LN(num_ratings), 3); ranked adds ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY score DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id). The outer query keeps genre_rank <= 5 and maps the rank to a verdict with CASE.

WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.title, m.genre1, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action', 'Comedy', 'Drama', 'Crime', 'Adventure')), scored AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, ROUND(avg_rating + 0.3 * LN(num_ratings), 3) AS score FROM eligible), ranked AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, score, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY score DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM scored) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings, score, CASE WHEN genre_rank <= 2 THEN 'Greenlight' WHEN genre_rank <= 5 THEN 'Develop' ELSE 'Hold' END AS verdict FROM ranked WHERE genre_rank <= 5 ORDER BY genre1, genre_rank.

```sql
WITH eligible AS (SELECT m.movie_id, m.title, m.genre1, r.avg_rating, r.num_ratings FROM movies m JOIN ratings r ON m.movie_id = r.movie_id WHERE r.num_ratings >= 50 AND m.genre1 IN ('Action', 'Comedy', 'Drama', 'Crime', 'Adventure')), scored AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, ROUND(avg_rating + 0.3 * LN(num_ratings), 3) AS score FROM eligible), ranked AS (SELECT movie_id, title, genre1, avg_rating, num_ratings, score, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre1 ORDER BY score DESC, num_ratings DESC, title, movie_id) AS genre_rank FROM scored) SELECT genre1, title, avg_rating, num_ratings, score, CASE WHEN genre_rank <= 2 THEN 'Greenlight' WHEN genre_rank <= 5 THEN 'Develop' ELSE 'Hold' END AS verdict FROM ranked WHERE genre_rank <= 5 ORDER BY genre1, genre_rank
```
